topIssues.issue: explanations use generic 'this is because' transitions that feel formulaic rather than natural teaching language
explanation for option N cites an advanced stat (era) inappropriate for diff:1 (ages 6-8)
explanation for option N has awkward phrasing ('because the downside outweighs the potential gain')
all explanations focus on why not to do something rather than teaching the positive principle
all explanations end with 'since/because this approach doesn't match the situation' - generic filler that teaches nothing specific
explanation #1 is incomplete - 'the runner is still fast afterward' doesn't explain why throwing over is less effective than slide step
explanation #3 is incomplete - just states the count without explaining why pitchout is strategically poor here
explanations don't teach the steal breakeven concept that's tagged - should explain success rates, timing, risk/reward
options could be more distinct - 'throw over' and 'slide step' are often used together, not mutually exclusive
explanation 1 has broken capitalization ('mlb' instead of 'mlb')
explanation 2 has incomplete sentence structure ('this is because make him chase')
count-leverage concept for diff:3 should include more advanced analytics or pitch sequencing theory
explanation 3 awkwardly phrases the walk-off walk consequence
best answer choice could be debated - many mlb pitchers throw strikes on 3-2 with bases loaded
explanations are repetitive with 'this approach doesn't match the situation' appearing twice
explanation for swing option focuses on why it's wrong rather than teaching the principle
score shows home team down 2-1 but situation says 'down 2-1' which could confuse perspective
explanations 0 and 2 use identical phrasing ('since this approach doesn't match the situation'), reducing teaching value
explanation 3 lacks depth - just states the rule without teaching the underlying principle of why bunting is wrong here
rates don't reflect realistic success probabilities for a devastating slider pitcher in 0-2 count

worstScenarios.title: Cutoff Decision
worstScenarios.concept: The cutoff man relays accurate throws home — catch and throw, don't let it go
issues: Explanations are internally contradictory - they say 'this approach doesn't match the situation' for the BEST answer
Runner positions are inconsistent - description says 'runner from 1st trying to score' but only runner on 1st can't score on a hit
Situation setup is impossible - runner can't go from 1st to home on a single hit to left-center
Best answer explanation argues AGAINST the choice instead of FOR it
No explanation for why cutting to 3rd (option 3) addresses the trailing runner
strengths: Correctly identifies SS as the cutoff for left-center field hits
Rate distribution shows reasonable spread with best answer highest
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Change runner to 2nd base so they can actually score on hit to left-center. Rewrite explanations so each argues FOR/AGAINST that specific option, especially fix the best answer to explain why relay throw beats long throw.

worstScenarios.title: First Baseman's DP — Bases Loaded
worstScenarios.concept: Bases loaded, ball hit near first — throw home FIRST to keep the force, then get the out at first
issues: Critical error: rates array shows option 3 as best (85) but best field says option 2
Explanation 2 is factually wrong - stepping on first does NOT remove force at second base
The described 'best play' (option 2) would allow a run to score unnecessarily
Explanations contradict each other about which play prevents the run
Concept statement contradicts the designated best answer
strengths: Realistic game situation with clear strategic decision
Age-appropriate complexity for diff:3 players
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Change best to 3 (throw home first), fix explanation 2 to correctly state that stepping on first only removes force at first/home but not second, align all explanations to teach that home-first preserves the force and prevents the run

worstScenarios.title: Full Count Pressure
worstScenarios.concept: Pitch location beats velocity in pressure situations
issues: Explanation 1 has broken capitalization ('mLB' instead of 'MLB')
Explanation 2 has incomplete sentence structure ('This is because make him chase')
Count-leverage concept for diff:3 should include more advanced analytics or pitch sequencing theory
Explanation 3 awkwardly phrases the walk-off walk consequence
Best answer choice could be debated - many MLB pitchers throw strikes on 3-2 with bases loaded
strengths: High-pressure situation perfectly captures the difficulty of full-count decisions
Rate distribution correctly shows slider as best with realistic tempting alternatives
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Fix the typos and incomplete sentences in explanations. Consider reframing best answer to acknowledge that both fastball strikes and slider are valid MLB approaches, with slider slightly preferred due to whiff rate. Add more sophisticated analysis for diff:3 level.

worstScenarios.title: Runner on Second — Check Before Throwing
worstScenarios.concept: With a runner on 2nd, look him back before throwing to first — don't give a free base
issues: The best answer is marked as option 2 (throw to third) with rate 25, but option 3 (glance then throw to first) has rate 85 and is clearly the optimal play
Explanation for option 2 incorrectly suggests throwing to third is ever the right play here - with runner on 2nd only, you almost never throw to third on a grounder to 3B
The concept teaches force-vs-tag but this isn't about force/tag plays - it's about checking runners before making throws
Rate distribution violates rules: best answer (option 2) has lower rate (25) than a wrong answer (option 3 at 85)
strengths: Realistic game situation that happens frequently in youth baseball
Age-appropriate language and concepts for difficulty 1
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Change best answer to option 3 (glance then throw to first), adjust rates so option 3 has 85 and option 2 has 25, rewrite explanations to teach that checking runners prevents free bases, and change conceptTag to 'situational-awareness' or 'runner-awareness'

worstScenarios.title: Appeal Play — Missing a Base
worstScenarios.concept: The defense can appeal a missed base — always touch every bag, even in the heat of the play
issues: Explanations are poorly written with contradictory phrases like 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' that make no sense in context
Option 3 explanation contradicts itself - says appeals can happen anytime, then says 'since this approach doesn't match the situation'
Explanation quality is confusing and doesn't clearly teach why each option is right/wrong
Option 4 explanation is incomplete ('This is because the defense must appeal' doesn't finish the thought)
Rates don't match reality - 85% for best answer is too high for such an obscure rule
strengths: Teaches an important but often misunderstood rule about appeal plays
Scenario setup is realistic and clearly describes the situation
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite all explanations to clearly explain WHY each option is right/wrong. Remove the nonsensical 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' phrases. Lower the best answer rate to 60-70% to reflect that this is an obscure rule many players don't know.

worstScenarios.title: Delayed Steal
worstScenarios.concept: The delayed steal exploits defensive inattention — awareness over pure speed
issues: Explanations are fragmented and incomplete - they read like unfinished sentences with 'This is because' tacked on
Explanation structure is confusing and doesn't clearly argue for/against each option
No clear teaching of WHY nobody covers the bag in this specific situation (1-1 count, 1 out)
Description says 'middle infielders aren't covering' but doesn't explain if this is pre-pitch positioning or post-pitch movement
The delayed steal concept isn't properly explained - when exactly does the runner break?
strengths: Teaches advanced baserunning concept that rewards baseball IQ over pure speed
Realistic scenario that happens frequently in real games
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite all explanations as complete sentences that teach the concept. Example for option 0: 'Perfect timing! The delayed steal works when middle infielders don't hold runners close and the catcher relaxes after receiving the pitch. By breaking as the catcher lazily lobs the ball back, you catch the defense completely off guard - the pitcher can't make the play and nobody is covering third.'

worstScenarios.title: 0-2: Pitcher's Count (Batter)
worstScenarios.concept: Down 0-2, become a fighter — shorten your swing and protect the plate (.167 BA in 0-2)
issues: Explanations contain grammar errors ('This is because shorten up' and 'This is because be ready' are fragments)
Option 2 explanation contradicts the option - says 'swinging big leads to strikeouts' but that's what option 2 suggests doing
Rates don't match strategic value - option 2 (swing big) at 15 is too low for a 'tempting wrong answer'
Concept teaches batting average stat but doesn't explain the strategic WHY of protecting the plate
No clear tempting wrong answer in the 40-65 range as required
strengths: Excellent use of MLB statistics to support the teaching point
Scenario perfectly matches 'counts' category with specific 0-2 count strategy
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Fix grammar in explanations (remove 'This is because'), make option 3 (take a pitch) the tempting wrong answer at 45-50 rate, and expand concept to explain WHY protecting the plate works (contact increases chances, fouling extends at-bat)

worstScenarios.title: Relay Position
worstScenarios.concept: On relay throws, line up directly between the outfielder and the target base
issues: Explanations are incomplete/generic - they don't teach WHY each option is right/wrong (e.g., 'That's because get in a straight line' is a fragment)
Scenario describes 'deep fly ball' but situation shows count 0-0 with runner on 1st - a fly ball would be caught for an out, not a relay situation
2nd baseman relays right side throws, but scenario incorrectly states they relay from right field to home (SS handles that relay)
Explanations contain grammar errors and incomplete sentences that would confuse kids
strengths: Clear single concept about relay positioning
Age-appropriate difficulty level for 11+ players
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Change to a base hit to right field (not fly ball), have SS be the relay person for throws to home from right field, and rewrite explanations to teach WHY each positioning choice helps or hurts the relay throw

worstScenarios.title: Ground Ball to Short — Runner on First
worstScenarios.concept: With a runner on first and nobody out, the double play is always the first thought
issues: Option 2 mentions 'cutting off the throw home' but with runner only on 1st and 0 outs, there's no play at home
Explanations follow rigid 'This is because' structure that sounds unnatural and doesn't flow well
Option 1 explanation says 'flip to second' but shortstop would throw, not flip from that position
Explanation 2 incorrectly states throwing to first advances runner to second - runner on 1st is forced out at 2nd
Options 2 and 4 both involve the pitcher unrealistically - pitcher doesn't participate in this play
strengths: Double play is absolutely the correct strategic choice in this situation
Scenario presents a common game situation that young players will face
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Replace options 2 and 4 with realistic alternatives like 'Throw to first for the sure out' and 'Hold the ball and look the runner back'. Rewrite explanations to flow naturally without forced 'This is because' structure, and fix the factual error about runners advancing on throws to first.

worstScenarios.title: Bunt Against the Shift
worstScenarios.concept: Always adjust positioning when the batter shows bunt
issues: Explanations are poorly written with grammar errors and don't teach the underlying principles
Option 0 ('Sprint back to 3B position') is unclear - back to cover the base or back to normal fielding position?
Concept is too generic - doesn't address the specific strategic decision of abandoning a shift for bunt defense
Explanation 2 has typo 'yOU' and doesn't explain why pitcher can't cover this bunt
All explanations use generic phrases like 'doesn't address the situation' instead of teaching specific baseball strategy
strengths: Realistic modern baseball scenario addressing defensive shifts
Appropriate difficulty level for ages 12+ with advanced defensive concepts
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite all explanations to teach specific principles: (0) 'Sprint to cover the 3B line - when shifted right, you must recover quickly to defend against bunts exploiting the open side', (1) 'Staying shifted leaves the entire 3B line open - the batter is specifically bunting to exploit your positioning', (2) 'The pitcher must cover 1B on this play - with runner on 1st, pitcher can't abandon their coverage responsibility', (3) 'Charging from deep right side takes too long - the bunt will already be past 3B by the time you arrive'

worstScenarios.title: Pitching Change Timing
worstScenarios.concept: Have a short leash plan: let dominant pitchers continue but act on the first sign of trouble
issues: Best answer (index 3) is 'Go to the bullpen after he allows a baserunner' but explanation refers to it as index 1
Explanations are misaligned - they don't match the options they're supposed to explain
Two explanations use identical filler phrase 'think about what gives your team the best result here'
95 pitches through 6 innings is extremely high (15.8 per inning) - unrealistic for a 'great' performance
Option 3 'Let him face one more batter' is too similar to option 2 'Let him start the 7th'
strengths: Teaching valuable times-through-order concept with velocity drop as fatigue indicator
Realistic game situation with specific details (pitch count, velocity drop, score)
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Fix explanation array indexing so each explanation matches its option. Replace generic 'think about what gives your team the best result' with specific strategic reasoning. Lower pitch count to ~85 for more realistic 'great' performance.

worstScenarios.title: Double Play Chance
worstScenarios.concept: When a ground ball comes right to you, turn the double play yourself
issues: Critical error in best answer explanation: says 'This is because two outs!' when there are 0 outs
Option 2 explanation has confusing grammar: 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' is tacked on awkwardly
Option 3 explanation contradicts standard practice: SS should handle the pivot on balls hit to 2B side
Explanations don't teach the key principle of when to turn two yourself vs when to flip to SS
strengths: Realistic game situation with appropriate pressure (1-run game, Top 5)
Age-appropriate language and simple concepts for diff:1
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Fix the critical '2 outs' error in explanation 0. Rewrite all explanations to teach WHEN to turn two yourself (slow roller, ball right at you) vs flip to SS (hard hit ball, need quick release). Remove the awkward 'since this approach doesn't match' phrases.

worstScenarios.title: Runner Threatening to Steal
worstScenarios.concept: The slide step controls the running game without hurting pitch count
issues: All explanations end with 'since/because this approach doesn't match the situation' - generic filler that teaches nothing specific
Explanation #1 is incomplete - 'the runner is still fast afterward' doesn't explain why throwing over is less effective than slide step
Explanation #3 is incomplete - just states the count without explaining why pitchout is strategically poor here
Explanations don't teach the steal breakeven concept that's tagged - should explain success rates, timing, risk/reward
Options could be more distinct - 'throw over' and 'slide step' are often used together, not mutually exclusive
strengths: Realistic game situation with appropriate pressure (1-run game, speedster on base)
Slide step timing detail (1.5s to 1.2s) provides concrete teaching value
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to teach specific lessons: #1 explain pickoff success rates vs speedsters, #2 explain how 0.3s saves 7-8 feet on steal attempts, #3 explain count leverage and why 2-0 hurts pitcher, #4 explain steal success rates with full windup vs slide step

worstScenarios.title: First Base Fundamentals
worstScenarios.concept: First baseman: get to the bag early, face the thrower, stretch toward the throw
issues: Explanations are poorly written with incomplete sentences ('This is because textbook' and 'This is why this approach matters' say nothing)
Explanations don't teach the underlying principles - they just state facts without explaining WHY
The scenario is too obvious for meaningful teaching - even 6-year-olds know to go to the bag
Rates don't match reality - option 2 (moving toward ball) would be higher than 20 in real play
strengths: Baseball situation is factually accurate with correct positioning
Age-appropriate language and concept for diff:1
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite all explanations to teach WHY each choice matters. For example: 'Perfect! Getting to the bag early lets you stretch for wild throws and gives the shortstop a clear target. The earlier you're set, the more time you have to adjust to bad throws.'

worstScenarios.title: Ball in the Sun
worstScenarios.concept: Use your glove to shade the sun — fundamental outfield technique
issues: All wrong answer explanations end with identical phrase 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' - repetitive and unhelpful
Best answer explanation has grammar error: 'This is because practice it every spring'
Explanations don't teach the WHY - they just state facts without explaining the principle
No explanation mentions the critical runner on first who could advance on a misplay
Scenario doesn't teach fly ball priority as tagged - it teaches sun management technique
strengths: Realistic game situation that every outfielder faces
Correct best answer - glove-up is standard MLB technique
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite all 4 explanations to: 1) Fix grammar in best answer, 2) Explain WHY each choice helps/hurts (e.g., glove blocks sun while keeping eyes on ball's path), 3) Mention game situation impact (runner advancing), 4) Remove repetitive endings

worstScenarios.title: Cold Off the Bench
worstScenarios.concept: As a pinch hitter, taking the first pitch to time the pitcher's velocity is usually smart — but stay ready
issues: Best answer is highly debatable - many hitting coaches would say swing if it's hittable in a do-or-die situation
Explanations 3 and 4 use identical generic text 'think about what gives your team the best result here'
Taking a pitch down 1 with 2 outs in the 9th reduces your chances to only 2 strikes remaining
Concept doesn't acknowledge the huge risk of falling behind 0-1 in this critical situation
strengths: Realistic high-pressure pinch hitting scenario that happens in real games
Options present genuinely different approaches to a difficult situation
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Change best answer to 'Swing if it's hittable' - with 2 outs and the tying run on 2nd, you can't afford to fall behind. Rewrite explanations to be unique and explain that while timing is important, the urgency of the situation outweighs the benefit of taking a pitch.

worstScenarios.title: Pinch Hitter Timing
worstScenarios.concept: Use pinch hitters in high-leverage spots — pitchers can't hit
issues: Explanations are poorly structured with awkward 'This is because' phrases that don't flow naturally
Option 2 explanation ('since this approach doesn't match the situation') is circular and doesn't teach anything
Difficulty 1 (ages 6-8) but uses advanced concepts like 'leverage-index' and batting average (.120)
Score shown as [2,4] but description says 'down 2' which would be [2,4] in top of inning, not bottom
strengths: Clear high-leverage situation that teaches when to make substitutions
Options present realistic managerial choices with good rate distribution
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to flow naturally without forced 'This is because' structure, simplify language for diff:1 (remove .120 batting average), and fix score to show home team ahead 4-2 in bottom 8th

worstScenarios.title: Lefty-Righty vs Switch Hitter
worstScenarios.concept: Against switch hitters, use your most effective reliever — handedness matters less than performance
issues: Best answer index is 3 but rates show index 3 (Bring in whoever has been pitching better) has highest rate of 85, creating index mismatch
Explanation for option 2 (It doesn't matter) contradicts itself by saying 'It matters a little' then explaining switch hitters have weaker sides
Rates don't follow proper distribution - best option should be 65-90 but is marked as 35, while index 3 has 85
Explanations 0 and 1 use confusing phrasing ('This is why this approach matters' and 'since this approach doesn't match') that doesn't clearly teach
Concept teaches to ignore platoon advantage entirely, but explanation admits switch hitters often have a weaker side
strengths: Realistic high-leverage situation that managers face with switch hitters
Age-appropriate complexity for diff:3 with advanced strategic thinking
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Fix the best answer index to 3 (matching the 85 rate), rewrite explanations to be clearer about why performance matters more than handedness against switch hitters, and adjust rates so index 2 gets 35 and index 3 gets 85 to match the best answer

worstScenarios.title: Jeter's Flip Play
worstScenarios.concept: Great plays happen when you anticipate — Jeter's hustle and instinct created the impossible
issues: Explanations are poorly written with awkward 'This is because' structure that doesn't flow naturally
Explanation for best answer focuses on the historical outcome rather than teaching the strategic principle
Score perspective is backwards: Bot 7 means HOME team bats, but Yankees (visiting team) were fielding in this play
No tempting wrong answer in the 40-65 range as required by rate distribution guidelines
strengths: Excellent use of a famous play to teach backup positioning and hustle
Options are distinct and represent realistic choices a shortstop might consider
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to flow naturally without 'This is because', fix the inning to 'Top 7' since Yankees were the visiting team, and adjust rates to include a tempting wrong answer around 50.

worstScenarios.title: Intentional Walk
worstScenarios.concept: Intentional walks set up force plays and let you face a weaker hitter
issues: Explanations 2-4 use generic 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' instead of teaching specific strategic principles
Option 3 'Walk both — load bases' is unrealistic - managers don't intentionally load bases down 1 in the 9th
Description lists .220 hitter has 8 HRs but explanation claims they 'strike out more' - contradictory characterization
Count shows 2-1 but scenario is about whether to intentionally walk, which ignores the existing count
Explanation quality is poor - only option 1 teaches a real principle while others are formulaic
strengths: Classic manager decision scenario teaching force play strategy
Rates realistically reflect that walking is the preferred strategic choice (80 vs 40)
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations 2-4 to teach specific principles: #2 could explain contact vs strikeout risk, #3 could discuss why loading bases increases scoring probability, #4 could teach about warm vs cold pitchers. Also clarify the .220 hitter profile - either high strikeouts OR 8 HRs, not both.

worstScenarios.title: Runner Trying to Score from First on a Double
worstScenarios.concept: On doubles off the wall, the relay throw is essential — no arm is strong enough to throw 350 feet accurately
issues: Explanation #3 has incomplete sentence: 'This is because stop the run first.' - grammatically incorrect and doesn't explain why throwing to third is wrong
Relay man positioning is vague - should specify shortstop is the relay on left side throws to home
Option 1 is misleading - left fielder should never attempt direct throw home from the wall, yet it has 40% rate suggesting it's somewhat viable
Explanation #4 generic platitude ('Never give up') rather than teaching why holding the ball is strategically wrong
strengths: Excellent teaching scenario for relay throw fundamentals with realistic game situation
Best answer correctly identifies relay throw as optimal strategy
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to be complete sentences that teach specific principles. For #3: 'Throwing to third lets the tying run score. Your priority is preventing runs, not holding runners.' For #4: 'Holding the ball guarantees the run scores. A good relay throw at least gives your team a chance at the out.'

worstScenarios.title: Squeeze Play Defense
worstScenarios.concept: The best squeeze defense is pitching high and inside — it's nearly impossible to bunt
issues: Explanation for option 2 contradicts baseball fundamentals — catchers DO charge bunts on squeeze plays when fielding the ball
Rates don't match explanations — option 4 has 85 rate but explanation says 'reacting is too passive', suggesting option 3 is better
Best answer index (3) doesn't align with highest rate option (index 3 is option 4, which has 85 rate)
Explanation 3 contradicts the scenario setup which says the catcher has 'spotted the squeeze coming early'
Count is 1-0 which is unusual for attempting a squeeze play — typically happens on 0-0, 1-0, or 2-1
strengths: Excellent teaching of high-and-tight pitch as squeeze defense
Realistic game situation with appropriate stakes for a squeeze attempt
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Fix the best answer index to match option 4 (index 3), clarify that catchers DO charge squeeze bunts when fielding, and adjust explanation 3 to support why high-and-tight is better than passive reaction

worstScenarios.title: Merkle's Boner
worstScenarios.concept: Always touch the next base — Merkle's mistake in 1908 changed a pennant race
issues: Bottom 9th with HOME team batting means Giants (home) would be at 1-1, not necessarily a walk-off situation as described
Rates don't match difficulty level - 90 for best answer is too high for a tricky historical rule situation
Explanations don't teach the broader force-out principle clearly enough for ages 9-12
Option 3 explanation uses 'This is because' awkwardly without completing the teaching point
strengths: Excellent use of famous play to teach force-out rules
Creates realistic tension between celebrating and completing the play
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Lower best answer rate to 70-75 to reflect the non-obvious nature of this rule. Revise explanations to better teach WHY force outs work this way, not just state what happened to Merkle.

worstScenarios.title: First Batter of the Game
worstScenarios.concept: Getting ahead in the count is a pitcher's biggest advantage
issues: Explanations use generic 'This is because' transitions that feel formulaic rather than natural teaching language
Explanation for option 1 cites an advanced stat (ERA) inappropriate for diff:1 (ages 6-8)
Explanation for option 2 has awkward phrasing ('because the downside outweighs the potential gain')
All explanations focus on why not to do something rather than teaching the positive principle
strengths: Scenario correctly teaches the fundamental concept of first-pitch strikes
Rates appropriately reflect that first-pitch strike is overwhelmingly the best choice (85)
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations using simpler language for 6-year-olds and focus on teaching WHY first-pitch strikes help rather than why other choices are wrong. Example: 'Perfect choice! When you throw strike one, the batter has to protect the plate. Now you can throw your other pitches and he might swing at balls!'

worstScenarios.title: Do-or-Die Play
worstScenarios.concept: In do-or-die plays: field cleanly, set your feet, throw accurately
issues: Explanations 2 and 3 use identical filler phrase 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' - not teaching specific principles
Explanation 1 contains circular reasoning ('This is why this approach matters in this situation')
Option 2 'hit cutoff' contradicts centerfielder's actual role - CF throws TO relay/cutoff, not hits them
Rates don't reflect realistic probabilities - 85% success for a game-ending throw from CF is too high
strengths: Excellent high-pressure scenario that teaches crucial decision-making
Age-appropriate difficulty 3 with proper complexity
worstScenarios.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to teach specific principles: #1 explain WHY setting feet improves accuracy under pressure, #2 explain biomechanics of why running throws lose accuracy, #3 change option to 'Field and throw to relay man' and explain time cost of relay on walkoff play

results.title: First Batter of the Game
results.concept: Getting ahead in the count is a pitcher's biggest advantage
issues: Explanations use generic 'This is because' transitions that feel formulaic rather than natural teaching language
Explanation for option 1 cites an advanced stat (ERA) inappropriate for diff:1 (ages 6-8)
Explanation for option 2 has awkward phrasing ('because the downside outweighs the potential gain')
All explanations focus on why not to do something rather than teaching the positive principle
strengths: Scenario correctly teaches the fundamental concept of first-pitch strikes
Rates appropriately reflect that first-pitch strike is overwhelmingly the best choice (85)
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations using simpler language for 6-year-olds and focus on teaching WHY first-pitch strikes help rather than why other choices are wrong. Example: 'Perfect choice! When you throw strike one, the batter has to protect the plate. Now you can throw your other pitches and he might swing at balls!'

results.title: Runner Threatening to Steal
results.concept: The slide step controls the running game without hurting pitch count
issues: All explanations end with 'since/because this approach doesn't match the situation' - generic filler that teaches nothing specific
Explanation #1 is incomplete - 'the runner is still fast afterward' doesn't explain why throwing over is less effective than slide step
Explanation #3 is incomplete - just states the count without explaining why pitchout is strategically poor here
Explanations don't teach the steal breakeven concept that's tagged - should explain success rates, timing, risk/reward
Options could be more distinct - 'throw over' and 'slide step' are often used together, not mutually exclusive
strengths: Realistic game situation with appropriate pressure (1-run game, speedster on base)
Slide step timing detail (1.5s to 1.2s) provides concrete teaching value
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to teach specific lessons: #1 explain pickoff success rates vs speedsters, #2 explain how 0.3s saves 7-8 feet on steal attempts, #3 explain count leverage and why 2-0 hurts pitcher, #4 explain steal success rates with full windup vs slide step

results.title: Full Count Pressure
results.concept: Pitch location beats velocity in pressure situations
issues: Explanation 1 has broken capitalization ('mLB' instead of 'MLB')
Explanation 2 has incomplete sentence structure ('This is because make him chase')
Count-leverage concept for diff:3 should include more advanced analytics or pitch sequencing theory
Explanation 3 awkwardly phrases the walk-off walk consequence
Best answer choice could be debated - many MLB pitchers throw strikes on 3-2 with bases loaded
strengths: High-pressure situation perfectly captures the difficulty of full-count decisions
Rate distribution correctly shows slider as best with realistic tempting alternatives
results.suggestedFix: Fix the typos and incomplete sentences in explanations. Consider reframing best answer to acknowledge that both fastball strikes and slider are valid MLB approaches, with slider slightly preferred due to whiff rate. Add more sophisticated analysis for diff:3 level.

results.title: RBI Opportunity
results.concept: With runner on 3rd, less than 2 outs: a fly ball is a productive out
issues: Explanations are repetitive with 'this approach doesn't match the situation' appearing twice
Explanation for swing option focuses on why it's wrong rather than teaching the principle
Score shows home team down 2-1 but situation says 'down 2-1' which could confuse perspective
strengths: Teaches fundamental RBI concept clearly
Perfect example of productive out strategy
Age-appropriate language for diff:1

results.title: Two-Strike Approach
results.concept: With 2 strikes, shorten your swing and focus on putting the ball in play
issues: Explanations 0 and 2 use identical phrasing ('since this approach doesn't match the situation'), reducing teaching value
Explanation 3 lacks depth - just states the rule without teaching the underlying principle of why bunting is wrong here
Rates don't reflect realistic success probabilities for a devastating slider pitcher in 0-2 count
strengths: Excellent two-strike teaching scenario with proper strategic emphasis on plate protection
Clear demonstration of why choking up and battling is superior to guessing in 0-2 counts
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations 0 and 2 to teach different principles (e.g., 'Guessing slider leaves you helpless against fastballs' and 'Looking fastball against a dominant slider means you'll be late adjusting'). Expand explanation 3 to teach why surprise plays fail with 2 strikes.

results.title: Cold Off the Bench
results.concept: As a pinch hitter, taking the first pitch to time the pitcher's velocity is usually smart — but stay ready
issues: Best answer is highly debatable - many hitting coaches would say swing if it's hittable in a do-or-die situation
Explanations 3 and 4 use identical generic text 'think about what gives your team the best result here'
Taking a pitch down 1 with 2 outs in the 9th reduces your chances to only 2 strikes remaining
Concept doesn't acknowledge the huge risk of falling behind 0-1 in this critical situation
strengths: Realistic high-pressure pinch hitting scenario that happens in real games
Options present genuinely different approaches to a difficult situation
results.suggestedFix: Change best answer to 'Swing if it's hittable' - with 2 outs and the tying run on 2nd, you can't afford to fall behind. Rewrite explanations to be unique and explain that while timing is important, the urgency of the situation outweighs the benefit of taking a pitch.

results.title: Double Play Chance
results.concept: When a ground ball comes right to you, turn the double play yourself
issues: Critical error in best answer explanation: says 'This is because two outs!' when there are 0 outs
Option 2 explanation has confusing grammar: 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' is tacked on awkwardly
Option 3 explanation contradicts standard practice: SS should handle the pivot on balls hit to 2B side
Explanations don't teach the key principle of when to turn two yourself vs when to flip to SS
strengths: Realistic game situation with appropriate pressure (1-run game, Top 5)
Age-appropriate language and simple concepts for diff:1
results.suggestedFix: Fix the critical '2 outs' error in explanation 0. Rewrite all explanations to teach WHEN to turn two yourself (slow roller, ball right at you) vs flip to SS (hard hit ball, need quick release). Remove the awkward 'since this approach doesn't match' phrases.

results.title: Relay Position
results.concept: On relay throws, line up directly between the outfielder and the target base
issues: Explanations are incomplete/generic - they don't teach WHY each option is right/wrong (e.g., 'That's because get in a straight line' is a fragment)
Scenario describes 'deep fly ball' but situation shows count 0-0 with runner on 1st - a fly ball would be caught for an out, not a relay situation
2nd baseman relays right side throws, but scenario incorrectly states they relay from right field to home (SS handles that relay)
Explanations contain grammar errors and incomplete sentences that would confuse kids
strengths: Clear single concept about relay positioning
Age-appropriate difficulty level for 11+ players
results.suggestedFix: Change to a base hit to right field (not fly ball), have SS be the relay person for throws to home from right field, and rewrite explanations to teach WHY each positioning choice helps or hurts the relay throw

results.title: Fast Runner — Can You Turn Two?
results.concept: Against very fast runners, get the sure out at second — don't force a double play and risk an error
issues: Explanation 0 has confusing logic - states 'two men on, nobody out is much worse' but scenario shows only one runner on
Explanation 2 ends awkwardly with 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' without explaining why
Option 3 'Skip the flip — throw directly to first' would leave the lead runner safe at second, creating a worse situation than trying for the DP
strengths: Teaches valuable real-game judgment about when NOT to force a double play
Correctly emphasizes taking the sure out against speedy runners
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to be clearer: Explanation 0 should say 'Rushing the relay throw risks an error, putting two runners on with nobody out.' Explanation 2 needs completion. Consider making option 3 more realistic.

results.title: Ground Ball to Short — Runner on First
results.concept: With a runner on first and nobody out, the double play is always the first thought
issues: Option 2 mentions 'cutting off the throw home' but with runner only on 1st and 0 outs, there's no play at home
Explanations follow rigid 'This is because' structure that sounds unnatural and doesn't flow well
Option 1 explanation says 'flip to second' but shortstop would throw, not flip from that position
Explanation 2 incorrectly states throwing to first advances runner to second - runner on 1st is forced out at 2nd
Options 2 and 4 both involve the pitcher unrealistically - pitcher doesn't participate in this play
strengths: Double play is absolutely the correct strategic choice in this situation
Scenario presents a common game situation that young players will face
results.suggestedFix: Replace options 2 and 4 with realistic alternatives like 'Throw to first for the sure out' and 'Hold the ball and look the runner back'. Rewrite explanations to flow naturally without forced 'This is because' structure, and fix the factual error about runners advancing on throws to first.

results.title: Cutoff Decision
results.concept: The cutoff man relays accurate throws home — catch and throw, don't let it go
issues: Explanations are internally contradictory - they say 'this approach doesn't match the situation' for the BEST answer
Runner positions are inconsistent - description says 'runner from 1st trying to score' but only runner on 1st can't score on a hit
Situation setup is impossible - runner can't go from 1st to home on a single hit to left-center
Best answer explanation argues AGAINST the choice instead of FOR it
No explanation for why cutting to 3rd (option 3) addresses the trailing runner
strengths: Correctly identifies SS as the cutoff for left-center field hits
Rate distribution shows reasonable spread with best answer highest
results.suggestedFix: Change runner to 2nd base so they can actually score on hit to left-center. Rewrite explanations so each argues FOR/AGAINST that specific option, especially fix the best answer to explain why relay throw beats long throw.

results.title: Cutoff Man Position on a Relay
results.concept: As the relay man, line up between the outfielder and home plate — give the outfielder a clear target
issues: Explanations use 'This is because' structure which sounds robotic rather than conversational teaching
Third explanation transitions awkwardly mid-sentence from stating the issue to explaining relay distance
strengths: Correctly identifies shortstop as relay man on left-center gap double with proper positioning mechanics
Teaches critical relay fundamentals including arm raising, chest-high target, and catch-and-fire technique

results.title: Pop Fly Priority
results.concept: Center fielder has priority on all fly balls — call it early and loud!
issues: Explanation doesn't address why rate is 90% instead of 100% - what 10% of cases would CF not take this?
Could mention that with runner on 2nd, preventing advancement is extra important
strengths: Correctly teaches CF priority rule that many youth players don't know
Age-appropriate language while teaching important safety concept

results.title: Extra Base Hit to the Gap
results.concept: On balls in the gap, use the relay man — two short throws beat one long throw
issues: Explanations use weak 'This is because' structure that sounds robotic rather than teaching naturally
Rate for option 1 (25) seems low for the 'hero ball' temptation that many youth players would attempt
strengths: Excellent example of relay throw fundamentals with clear game situation
Options include realistic temptations (throw home, throw to third) that youth players commonly choose incorrectly
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to flow more naturally: 'Throwing home from the gap means a 300+ foot throw that will bounce multiple times, giving the batter extra bases' instead of the stilted 'This is because' structure

results.title: Do-or-Die Play
results.concept: In do-or-die plays: field cleanly, set your feet, throw accurately
issues: Explanations 2 and 3 use identical filler phrase 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' - not teaching specific principles
Explanation 1 contains circular reasoning ('This is why this approach matters in this situation')
Option 2 'hit cutoff' contradicts centerfielder's actual role - CF throws TO relay/cutoff, not hits them
Rates don't reflect realistic probabilities - 85% success for a game-ending throw from CF is too high
strengths: Excellent high-pressure scenario that teaches crucial decision-making
Age-appropriate difficulty 3 with proper complexity
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to teach specific principles: #1 explain WHY setting feet improves accuracy under pressure, #2 explain biomechanics of why running throws lose accuracy, #3 change option to 'Field and throw to relay man' and explain time cost of relay on walkoff play

results.title: Ball in the Sun
results.concept: Use your glove to shade the sun — fundamental outfield technique
issues: All wrong answer explanations end with identical phrase 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' - repetitive and unhelpful
Best answer explanation has grammar error: 'This is because practice it every spring'
Explanations don't teach the WHY - they just state facts without explaining the principle
No explanation mentions the critical runner on first who could advance on a misplay
Scenario doesn't teach fly ball priority as tagged - it teaches sun management technique
strengths: Realistic game situation that every outfielder faces
Correct best answer - glove-up is standard MLB technique
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite all 4 explanations to: 1) Fix grammar in best answer, 2) Explain WHY each choice helps/hurts (e.g., glove blocks sun while keeping eyes on ball's path), 3) Mention game situation impact (runner advancing), 4) Remove repetitive endings

results.title: Runner Trying to Score from First on a Double
results.concept: On doubles off the wall, the relay throw is essential — no arm is strong enough to throw 350 feet accurately
issues: Explanation #3 has incomplete sentence: 'This is because stop the run first.' - grammatically incorrect and doesn't explain why throwing to third is wrong
Relay man positioning is vague - should specify shortstop is the relay on left side throws to home
Option 1 is misleading - left fielder should never attempt direct throw home from the wall, yet it has 40% rate suggesting it's somewhat viable
Explanation #4 generic platitude ('Never give up') rather than teaching why holding the ball is strategically wrong
strengths: Excellent teaching scenario for relay throw fundamentals with realistic game situation
Best answer correctly identifies relay throw as optimal strategy
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to be complete sentences that teach specific principles. For #3: 'Throwing to third lets the tying run score. Your priority is preventing runs, not holding runners.' For #4: 'Holding the ball guarantees the run scores. A good relay throw at least gives your team a chance at the out.'

results.title: Wind Blowing Out — Play Deeper
results.concept: When the wind blows out, play deeper — fly balls carry farther and you need the extra room
issues: Explanations 2-4 have incomplete sentences with 'This is because' fragments that don't flow properly
The scenario doesn't explicitly state what position the player is (left fielder) until you infer from metadata
For difficulty 3, the explanations could include more advanced concepts like specific depth adjustments based on batter tendencies
strengths: Excellent real-world scenario that teaches crucial outfield positioning based on weather conditions
Rate distribution perfectly reflects realistic decision-making with a clear best answer and tempting alternative
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations 2-4 to complete the sentences properly: 'Adjusting on each ball means you're constantly behind because you need to set your depth before the pitch.' Also clarify in the description that 'you're the left fielder.'

results.title: First Base Fundamentals
results.concept: First baseman: get to the bag early, face the thrower, stretch toward the throw
issues: Explanations are poorly written with incomplete sentences ('This is because textbook' and 'This is why this approach matters' say nothing)
Explanations don't teach the underlying principles - they just state facts without explaining WHY
The scenario is too obvious for meaningful teaching - even 6-year-olds know to go to the bag
Rates don't match reality - option 2 (moving toward ball) would be higher than 20 in real play
strengths: Baseball situation is factually accurate with correct positioning
Age-appropriate language and concept for diff:1
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite all explanations to teach WHY each choice matters. For example: 'Perfect! Getting to the bag early lets you stretch for wild throws and gives the shortstop a clear target. The earlier you're set, the more time you have to adjust to bad throws.'

results.title: Pull Hitter — Shade or Stay?
results.concept: Against pull hitters, shade the infield toward the pull side — position where the ball is going
issues: Explanation transitions could be smoother - some use 'because' awkwardly in the middle of sentences
Option 3 (only shade second baseman) might be too obviously wrong for diff:2
strengths: Excellent teaching of positioning adjustments based on hitter tendencies
Clear progression from basic shading to extreme shifts
Age-appropriate complexity that teaches data-driven defense

results.title: First Baseman's DP — Bases Loaded
results.concept: Bases loaded, ball hit near first — throw home FIRST to keep the force, then get the out at first
issues: Critical error: rates array shows option 3 as best (85) but best field says option 2
Explanation 2 is factually wrong - stepping on first does NOT remove force at second base
The described 'best play' (option 2) would allow a run to score unnecessarily
Explanations contradict each other about which play prevents the run
Concept statement contradicts the designated best answer
strengths: Realistic game situation with clear strategic decision
Age-appropriate complexity for diff:3 players
results.suggestedFix: Change best to 3 (throw home first), fix explanation 2 to correctly state that stepping on first only removes force at first/home but not second, align all explanations to teach that home-first preserves the force and prevents the run

results.title: Backing Up Bases
results.concept: Always back up the base where the throw is going — anticipate overthrows
issues: Explanations 2 and 3 end with generic 'think about what gives your team the best result' instead of explaining the specific positioning principle
Explanation 0 has awkward phrasing ('since this approach doesn't match the situation')
Concept doesn't match the scenario - says 'back up where the throw is going' but throw is going to 1st, not 3rd where runner is heading
strengths: Correctly teaches right fielder's backup responsibility on routine grounders
Age-appropriate difficulty and language for 8+ year olds
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to be specific: explain that RF backs up 1st on grounders to the left side, LF backs up 3rd, and why each position has these assignments based on throwing angles

results.title: Shallow Fly Tag-Up
results.concept: On shallow fly balls, throw directly home — the short distance is your advantage
issues: Score perspective error: Bot 8 with 2-3 score means HOME team (batting) is losing, not winning as description implies
strengths: Excellent explanation quality that teaches the WHY behind each choice
Perfect use of shallow fly ball mechanics to teach direct throw advantage

results.title: Ball at the Wall — Make the Play or Protect Yourself?
results.concept: At the wall, use your free hand to find and brace against it while catching with your glove hand
issues: Option 3 ('Reach up and try to time the catch before impact') is vague and unclear about the actual technique being suggested
Rate for option 1 (30%) seems too low for 'making the catch to win the game' - many players would choose this despite the risk
strengths: Excellent teaching of proper wall technique that balances safety with competitive play
Explanations provide clear reasoning for each option while teaching the underlying principle

results.title: Runner on Second — Check Before Throwing
results.concept: With a runner on 2nd, look him back before throwing to first — don't give a free base
issues: The best answer is marked as option 2 (throw to third) with rate 25, but option 3 (glance then throw to first) has rate 85 and is clearly the optimal play
Explanation for option 2 incorrectly suggests throwing to third is ever the right play here - with runner on 2nd only, you almost never throw to third on a grounder to 3B
The concept teaches force-vs-tag but this isn't about force/tag plays - it's about checking runners before making throws
Rate distribution violates rules: best answer (option 2) has lower rate (25) than a wrong answer (option 3 at 85)
strengths: Realistic game situation that happens frequently in youth baseball
Age-appropriate language and concepts for difficulty 1
results.suggestedFix: Change best answer to option 3 (glance then throw to first), adjust rates so option 3 has 85 and option 2 has 25, rewrite explanations to teach that checking runners prevents free bases, and change conceptTag to 'situational-awareness' or 'runner-awareness'

results.title: Bare-Hand Roller
results.concept: On dying rollers, bare-handing saves the glove transfer time needed to beat fast runners on the long throw to first.
issues: Explanations use repetitive 'This is because' structure that sounds formulaic
Option 3 (flip to shortstop) is unrealistic - shortstop wouldn't be covering on this play
strengths: Teaches a fundamental third base skill that young players need to master
Excellent rate distribution shows clear best answer while having a tempting alternative
Situation perfectly sets up the need for bare-handing technique

results.title: Bunt Against the Shift
results.concept: Always adjust positioning when the batter shows bunt
issues: Explanations are poorly written with grammar errors and don't teach the underlying principles
Option 0 ('Sprint back to 3B position') is unclear - back to cover the base or back to normal fielding position?
Concept is too generic - doesn't address the specific strategic decision of abandoning a shift for bunt defense
Explanation 2 has typo 'yOU' and doesn't explain why pitcher can't cover this bunt
All explanations use generic phrases like 'doesn't address the situation' instead of teaching specific baseball strategy
strengths: Realistic modern baseball scenario addressing defensive shifts
Appropriate difficulty level for ages 12+ with advanced defensive concepts
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite all explanations to teach specific principles: (0) 'Sprint to cover the 3B line - when shifted right, you must recover quickly to defend against bunts exploiting the open side', (1) 'Staying shifted leaves the entire 3B line open - the batter is specifically bunting to exploit your positioning', (2) 'The pitcher must cover 1B on this play - with runner on 1st, pitcher can't abandon their coverage responsibility', (3) 'Charging from deep right side takes too long - the bunt will already be past 3B by the time you arrive'

results.title: Stealing Second
results.concept: Steal when conditions align: slow pitcher, weak catcher, hitter's count
issues: Explanations use specific MLB success percentages (85%+) that may not apply to youth baseball where this scenario is targeted
The 72% breakeven threshold is mentioned multiple times but never explained why that specific number matters
strengths: Excellent teaching of the three-condition framework for stealing (pitcher speed, catcher arm, count)
Clear strategic concept that applies across all age levels
Explanations differentiate well between options while teaching the underlying principle

results.title: First-to-Third
results.concept: Round bases aggressively and trust your 3rd base coach
issues: Explanations are too generic and don't teach the specific principles of reading the ball off the bat or understanding right field angles
Count (1-1) is irrelevant for a baserunning scenario after the ball is hit
Explanation structure uses awkward transitions ('This is because', 'since', 'which means') that sound unnatural
strengths: Correctly identifies rounding second and reading the coach as the best baserunning approach
Rate distribution is realistic with the aggressive play rated highest
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to teach specific principles: explain WHY the 3rd base coach has the best view, describe the right field single angle advantage, and remove the awkward transitional phrases

results.title: Delayed Steal
results.concept: The delayed steal exploits defensive inattention — awareness over pure speed
issues: Explanations are fragmented and incomplete - they read like unfinished sentences with 'This is because' tacked on
Explanation structure is confusing and doesn't clearly argue for/against each option
No clear teaching of WHY nobody covers the bag in this specific situation (1-1 count, 1 out)
Description says 'middle infielders aren't covering' but doesn't explain if this is pre-pitch positioning or post-pitch movement
The delayed steal concept isn't properly explained - when exactly does the runner break?
strengths: Teaches advanced baserunning concept that rewards baseball IQ over pure speed
Realistic scenario that happens frequently in real games
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite all explanations as complete sentences that teach the concept. Example for option 0: 'Perfect timing! The delayed steal works when middle infielders don't hold runners close and the catcher relaxes after receiving the pitch. By breaking as the catcher lazily lobs the ball back, you catch the defense completely off guard - the pitcher can't make the play and nobody is covering third.'

results.title: Pinch Hitter Timing
results.concept: Use pinch hitters in high-leverage spots — pitchers can't hit
issues: Explanations are poorly structured with awkward 'This is because' phrases that don't flow naturally
Option 2 explanation ('since this approach doesn't match the situation') is circular and doesn't teach anything
Difficulty 1 (ages 6-8) but uses advanced concepts like 'leverage-index' and batting average (.120)
Score shown as [2,4] but description says 'down 2' which would be [2,4] in top of inning, not bottom
strengths: Clear high-leverage situation that teaches when to make substitutions
Options present realistic managerial choices with good rate distribution
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to flow naturally without forced 'This is because' structure, simplify language for diff:1 (remove .120 batting average), and fix score to show home team ahead 4-2 in bottom 8th

results.title: Intentional Walk
results.concept: Intentional walks set up force plays and let you face a weaker hitter
issues: Explanations 2-4 use generic 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' instead of teaching specific strategic principles
Option 3 'Walk both — load bases' is unrealistic - managers don't intentionally load bases down 1 in the 9th
Description lists .220 hitter has 8 HRs but explanation claims they 'strike out more' - contradictory characterization
Count shows 2-1 but scenario is about whether to intentionally walk, which ignores the existing count
Explanation quality is poor - only option 1 teaches a real principle while others are formulaic
strengths: Classic manager decision scenario teaching force play strategy
Rates realistically reflect that walking is the preferred strategic choice (80 vs 40)
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations 2-4 to teach specific principles: #2 could explain contact vs strikeout risk, #3 could discuss why loading bases increases scoring probability, #4 could teach about warm vs cold pitchers. Also clarify the .220 hitter profile - either high strikeouts OR 8 HRs, not both.

results.title: Pitching Change Timing
results.concept: Have a short leash plan: let dominant pitchers continue but act on the first sign of trouble
issues: Best answer (index 3) is 'Go to the bullpen after he allows a baserunner' but explanation refers to it as index 1
Explanations are misaligned - they don't match the options they're supposed to explain
Two explanations use identical filler phrase 'think about what gives your team the best result here'
95 pitches through 6 innings is extremely high (15.8 per inning) - unrealistic for a 'great' performance
Option 3 'Let him face one more batter' is too similar to option 2 'Let him start the 7th'
strengths: Teaching valuable times-through-order concept with velocity drop as fatigue indicator
Realistic game situation with specific details (pitch count, velocity drop, score)
results.suggestedFix: Fix explanation array indexing so each explanation matches its option. Replace generic 'think about what gives your team the best result' with specific strategic reasoning. Lower pitch count to ~85 for more realistic 'great' performance.

results.title: Blocking a Pitch in the Dirt
results.concept: Block first, catch second — keep your body centered behind the ball in the dirt
issues: Explanations follow awkward 'This is because' pattern that sounds unnatural
Option 3 'Stay standing and swipe at it' is unrealistically bad for any trained catcher
Scenario teaches physical technique (HOW to block) rather than strategic decision-making
strengths: High-pressure situation with runner on 3rd makes the blocking technique crucial
Correct blocking fundamentals accurately described in best option
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to flow naturally without forced 'This is because' structure, and consider making this a strategic decision (e.g., 'Do you block or try to catch cleanly for a tag play?') rather than pure technique instruction

results.title: Pitch Calling — Full Count
results.concept: Use a hitter's weakness even in full counts — pattern recognition wins
issues: Explanations follow formulaic 'This is because' structure that sounds unnatural
Explanation 1 argues against fastball but doesn't explain why slider is specifically better
Wild pitch risk mentioned for curveball applies equally to slider low and away
strengths: Excellent use of batter history (struck out twice on breaking balls) to justify strategy
Score perspective correct (Bot 5 = home team batting, down 3-2)
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to flow naturally without forced 'This is because' structure, and have explanation 1 advocate FOR the slider rather than just against the fastball

results.title: Squeeze Play Defense
results.concept: The best squeeze defense is pitching high and inside — it's nearly impossible to bunt
issues: Explanation for option 2 contradicts baseball fundamentals — catchers DO charge bunts on squeeze plays when fielding the ball
Rates don't match explanations — option 4 has 85 rate but explanation says 'reacting is too passive', suggesting option 3 is better
Best answer index (3) doesn't align with highest rate option (index 3 is option 4, which has 85 rate)
Explanation 3 contradicts the scenario setup which says the catcher has 'spotted the squeeze coming early'
Count is 1-0 which is unusual for attempting a squeeze play — typically happens on 0-0, 1-0, or 2-1
strengths: Excellent teaching of high-and-tight pitch as squeeze defense
Realistic game situation with appropriate stakes for a squeeze attempt
results.suggestedFix: Fix the best answer index to match option 4 (index 3), clarify that catchers DO charge squeeze bunts when fielding, and adjust explanation 3 to support why high-and-tight is better than passive reaction

results.title: The Pine Tar Game
results.concept: Knowing the rulebook gives you strategic options — Billy Martin's pine tar appeal was smart managing, even though the league later overturned it.
issues: Concept tag 'obstruction-interference' doesn't match the scenario content, which is about equipment violations and rules appeals
Situation shows Top 9, 2 outs, runner on first, but description says Brett hit a 'two-run homer' - this is mathematically impossible
Option 2 explanation focuses too much on what Martin did rather than teaching why this specific choice is wrong
The scenario teaches multiple concepts (equipment rules, appeal timing, managerial strategy) rather than one clear principle
strengths: Excellent use of real baseball history to teach strategic thinking and rules knowledge
Best answer explanation effectively teaches both what happened and the underlying principle about using rules strategically
results.suggestedFix: Fix the runner situation to match a two-run homer (runner on second or third), change conceptTag to 'rules-appeal' or 'equipment-rules', and focus explanations more on teaching the specific strategic principle of each choice.

results.title: Merkle's Boner
results.concept: Always touch the next base — Merkle's mistake in 1908 changed a pennant race
issues: Bottom 9th with HOME team batting means Giants (home) would be at 1-1, not necessarily a walk-off situation as described
Rates don't match difficulty level - 90 for best answer is too high for a tricky historical rule situation
Explanations don't teach the broader force-out principle clearly enough for ages 9-12
Option 3 explanation uses 'This is because' awkwardly without completing the teaching point
strengths: Excellent use of famous play to teach force-out rules
Creates realistic tension between celebrating and completing the play
results.suggestedFix: Lower best answer rate to 70-75 to reflect the non-obvious nature of this rule. Revise explanations to better teach WHY force outs work this way, not just state what happened to Merkle.

results.title: Jeter's Flip Play
results.concept: Great plays happen when you anticipate — Jeter's hustle and instinct created the impossible
issues: Explanations are poorly written with awkward 'This is because' structure that doesn't flow naturally
Explanation for best answer focuses on the historical outcome rather than teaching the strategic principle
Score perspective is backwards: Bot 7 means HOME team bats, but Yankees (visiting team) were fielding in this play
No tempting wrong answer in the 40-65 range as required by rate distribution guidelines
strengths: Excellent use of a famous play to teach backup positioning and hustle
Options are distinct and represent realistic choices a shortstop might consider
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to flow naturally without 'This is because', fix the inning to 'Top 7' since Yankees were the visiting team, and adjust rates to include a tempting wrong answer around 50.

results.title: Ground Rule Double
results.concept: Ground rule double: all runners advance exactly two bases — even if they could have scored
issues: Explanations for wrong answers end with generic phrases like 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' instead of teaching why
ConceptTag 'force-vs-tag' doesn't match the scenario content, which teaches ground rule double mechanics
Option 3 explanation is confusing - it seems to support the correct answer while supposedly explaining why option 3 is wrong
strengths: Excellent teaching of a commonly misunderstood rule that even adults often get wrong
Clear, age-appropriate language perfect for diff:1 learners
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to teach the principle behind each wrong answer. For example, option 1 should explain 'A home run must clear the fence without bouncing. Once the ball touches the ground, it can never be a home run.' Also change conceptTag to 'ground-rule-double'.

results.title: Infield Fly Rule
results.concept: Infield fly rule: batter is out, ball is live, runners advance at own risk — prevents trick double plays
issues: Explanation 2 is too brief and doesn't explain WHY the ball staying live is important
Explanation 3 has confusing phrasing ('since this approach doesn't match the situation')
Count of 1-0 is unusual for teaching infield fly rule (typically shown on 0-0 or full count)
strengths: Excellent teaching of a complex rule that confuses many young players
Perfect setup with runners on 1st and 2nd, 1 out for infield fly situation
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations 2 and 3 to be clearer: #2 should explain 'The ball stays live, meaning runners can try to advance after the ball drops, but they risk being thrown out.' #3 should say 'The fielder can let it drop without penalty — the batter is already out by rule.'

results.title: Appeal Play — Missing a Base
results.concept: The defense can appeal a missed base — always touch every bag, even in the heat of the play
issues: Explanations are poorly written with contradictory phrases like 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' that make no sense in context
Option 3 explanation contradicts itself - says appeals can happen anytime, then says 'since this approach doesn't match the situation'
Explanation quality is confusing and doesn't clearly teach why each option is right/wrong
Option 4 explanation is incomplete ('This is because the defense must appeal' doesn't finish the thought)
Rates don't match reality - 85% for best answer is too high for such an obscure rule
strengths: Teaches an important but often misunderstood rule about appeal plays
Scenario setup is realistic and clearly describes the situation
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite all explanations to clearly explain WHY each option is right/wrong. Remove the nonsensical 'since this approach doesn't match the situation' phrases. Lower the best answer rate to 60-70% to reflect that this is an obscure rule many players don't know.

results.title: 0-0: First Pitch Approach (Batter)
results.concept: Hitting 0-0: if you know what's coming, be aggressive — hitters bat .340 on first pitches
issues: Options 3 and 4 are both defensive/small-ball approaches that overlap conceptually
Explanation 2 could better explain WHY falling behind 0-1 is disadvantageous
The .340 statistic needs context - is this all first pitches or just those swung at?
strengths: Excellent use of MLB statistics to support the strategic principle
Clear teaching of pattern recognition and aggressive hitting philosophy
results.suggestedFix: Replace option 4 with a more distinct strategic choice like 'Look for a walk by being extra selective' to create clearer contrast between aggressive and passive approaches

results.title: 0-2: Pitcher's Count (Batter)
results.concept: Down 0-2, become a fighter — shorten your swing and protect the plate (.167 BA in 0-2)
issues: Explanations contain grammar errors ('This is because shorten up' and 'This is because be ready' are fragments)
Option 2 explanation contradicts the option - says 'swinging big leads to strikeouts' but that's what option 2 suggests doing
Rates don't match strategic value - option 2 (swing big) at 15 is too low for a 'tempting wrong answer'
Concept teaches batting average stat but doesn't explain the strategic WHY of protecting the plate
No clear tempting wrong answer in the 40-65 range as required
strengths: Excellent use of MLB statistics to support the teaching point
Scenario perfectly matches 'counts' category with specific 0-2 count strategy
results.suggestedFix: Fix grammar in explanations (remove 'This is because'), make option 3 (take a pitch) the tempting wrong answer at 45-50 rate, and expand concept to explain WHY protecting the plate works (contact increases chances, fouling extends at-bat)

results.title: Full Count: 3-2 with 2 Outs
results.concept: Full count discipline: be aggressively selective — swing at strikes, lay off balls, even with 2 strikes
issues: Explanations use generic filler phrases like 'since it doesn't address the situation correctly' instead of teaching specific principles
Explanation #2 has awkward phrasing with 'This is because' in the middle of the thought
The 'runner is going' detail conflicts with being down by 1 run in the 7th with 2 outs - runner would likely not be stealing
Rates don't align well with difficulty 3 - a 30% rate for 'swing at anything close' seems high for advanced players
strengths: Excellent teaching of count leverage and the 'aggressively selective' concept for full counts
Scenario correctly emphasizes discipline even with 2 strikes, which is advanced strategy
results.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanations to teach specific principles: #1 should explain how bad pitches lead to weak groundouts, #2 should explain zone discipline without awkward transitions, #3 should teach about umpire tendencies on 3-2, #4 should explain how overswinging increases strikeout risk

results.title: Infield Depth — Big Lead Changes the Calculus
results.concept: With a big lead, normal depth prioritizes outs over individual runs — don't bring the infield in when you can afford the run
issues: Explanation 3 starts with 'This is because' which appears to be a formatting error or incomplete sentence
Explanation 4 also has 'This is because' mid-explanation, creating awkward phrasing
strengths: Excellent teaching of how game context (lead size) changes defensive strategy
Clear contrast with the typical 'infield in' situation to show strategic flexibility

results.title: Tag the Runner at Third
results.concept: On headfirst slides, sweep the tag low in front of the bag — meet the runner's hands before they reach the base.
issues: Scenario describes a caught-in-rundown situation but teaches basic tagging technique instead
Runner was never on third base - started on second and was caught between bases
Concept doesn't match the complex rundown situation described
strengths: Excellent teaching of proper tag technique for headfirst slides
Age-appropriate language and clear explanations for young players
results.suggestedFix: Simplify scenario to match the tagging lesson: 'Runner on third tries to score on a wild pitch but retreats when the catcher recovers. He slides headfirst back to third as you receive the throw.'

results.title: Lefty-Righty vs Switch Hitter
results.concept: Against switch hitters, use your most effective reliever — handedness matters less than performance
issues: Best answer index is 3 but rates show index 3 (Bring in whoever has been pitching better) has highest rate of 85, creating index mismatch
Explanation for option 2 (It doesn't matter) contradicts itself by saying 'It matters a little' then explaining switch hitters have weaker sides
Rates don't follow proper distribution - best option should be 65-90 but is marked as 35, while index 3 has 85
Explanations 0 and 1 use confusing phrasing ('This is why this approach matters' and 'since this approach doesn't match') that doesn't clearly teach
Concept teaches to ignore platoon advantage entirely, but explanation admits switch hitters often have a weaker side
strengths: Realistic high-leverage situation that managers face with switch hitters
Age-appropriate complexity for diff:3 with advanced strategic thinking
results.suggestedFix: Fix the best answer index to 3 (matching the 85 rate), rewrite explanations to be clearer about why performance matters more than handedness against switch hitters, and adjust rates so index 2 gets 35 and index 3 gets 85 to match the best answer

results.title: Two Strikes — Breaking Ball Barrage
results.concept: After a breaking ball barrage, stay reactive — don't guess, let your eyes and hands adjust
issues: Explanation 1 ends abruptly with 'This is because he knows you've seen three curves' - feels incomplete
Rates might be slightly high for wrong answers (35, 30, 20) making the scenario too easy
strengths: Excellent two-strike teaching scenario that addresses a common youth baseball challenge
Best answer teaches the crucial 'see ball, hit ball' approach over guessing
Realistic game situation with appropriate pressure for the concept

results.title: Walk to Load Bases — Force at Home
results.concept: Loading the bases for a force at home can be smart — force outs at the plate are much easier than tags
issues: First base open claim is incorrect — runners are on 2nd and 3rd, so 1st is indeed open
Option 3 explanation dismisses matchup consideration too quickly when it's actually relevant in close games
Explanations 3 and 4 use weak negative framing instead of teaching positive principles
strengths: Excellent teaching of force vs tag play concept with clear practical application
Realistic high-pressure situation that demonstrates advanced strategic thinking
